claims.claim_text: Specific 2021 fintech breach story involving an etcd encryption key stored in a Kubernetes Secret, NFS backups, 340 microservices, and an 11-week detection gap.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: In 2021, a fintech company running Kubernetes on-premises stored their etcd encryption key in a Kubernetes Secret. That Secret was base64-encoded (not encrypted) and backed up nightly to an NFS share. A contractor with read access to the NFS share decoded the Secret, extracted the etcd encryption key, and used it to decrypt a backup of etcd -- which contained every Secret in the cluster: database passwords, API keys, TLS certificates, and service account tokens for 340 microservices. The breach was not detected for 11 weeks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A common failure mode in self-managed Kubernetes is storing the etcd encryption key alongside the data it protects or in broadly accessible backup systems; if an attacker obtains both, etcd backups can expose every Secret in the cluster.
claims.rationale: This is a named, date-specific incident with precise operational details but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: AWS KMS protects keys with hardware security modules and plaintext key material never leaves the HSM boundary.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: AWS KMS documentation directly states that KMS key material is protected by HSMs and plaintext key material never leaves the HSM boundary.

claims.claim_text: Network-appliance HSMs such as Thales Luna and Entrust nShield deliver 10,000+ ops/sec and cost $20K-$100K.
claims.span_hint: section: HSM Form Factors table, network appliance row
claims.anchor_text: | Network appliance | Thales Luna, Entrust nShield | 10,000+ ops/sec | $20K-$100K | Enterprise PKI, payment processing |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Network appliance | Enterprise HSM appliances | High throughput | High cost | Enterprise PKI, payment processing |
claims.rationale: The row mixes vendor examples with exact throughput and pricing ranges that are product- and configuration-dependent and not stably supported by a single primary source.

claims.claim_text: PCIe HSMs such as Thales Luna PCIe and Utimaco deliver 5,000+ ops/sec and cost $5K-$30K.
claims.span_hint: section: HSM Form Factors table, PCIe row
claims.anchor_text: | PCIe card | Thales Luna PCIe, Utimaco | 5,000+ ops/sec | $5K-$30K | Single server, Vault backend |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | PCIe card | PCIe HSMs | Moderate to high throughput | Lower cost than network appliances | Single server, Vault backend |
claims.rationale: The exact throughput and price bands are vendor- and model-specific and are not reliably backed here.

claims.claim_text: YubiHSM 2 delivers 50 ops/sec and costs $650.
claims.span_hint: section: HSM Form Factors table, USB token row
claims.anchor_text: | USB token | YubiHSM 2 | 50 ops/sec | $650 | Small deployments, dev/test |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | USB token | USB HSMs | Lower throughput | Much lower cost | Small deployments, dev/test |
claims.rationale: The exact performance and price are volatile product facts that should not be frozen into the table without a current vendor source.

claims.claim_text: AWS CloudHSM hsm2m.medium is FIPS 140-3 Level 3 certified and hsm1.medium moved to historical status on January 4, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: HSM Form Factors, paragraph after table
claims.rationale: AWS CloudHSM compliance documentation directly covers the hsm1.medium historical date and the current FIPS validation state.

claims.claim_text: Azure Cloud HSM is the successor to Azure Dedicated HSM.
claims.span_hint: section: HSM Form Factors, paragraph after table
claims.rationale: Microsoft Learn explicitly describes Azure Cloud HSM as the successor to Azure Dedicated HSM.

claims.claim_text: Azure Cloud HSM uses Marvell LiquidSecurity HSMs validated to FIPS 140-3 Level 3.
claims.span_hint: section: HSM Form Factors, paragraph after table
claims.rationale: Microsoft Learn service-limits documentation directly names the Marvell LiquidSecurity hardware and its FIPS validation level.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud HSM is backed by FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certified hardware.
claims.span_hint: section: HSM Form Factors, paragraph after table
claims.rationale: Google Cloud documentation directly states that Cloud HSM uses FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certified HSMs.

claims.claim_text: TPM 2.0 is standardized as ISO/IEC 11889:2015.
claims.span_hint: section: TPM for Measured Boot, first paragraph
claims.rationale: The ISO page directly identifies ISO/IEC 11889-1:2015 as the TPM architecture standard.

claims.claim_text: The TCG PC Client Platform Profile mandates SHA-1 and SHA-256 PCR banks, each containing 24 registers (PCR 0-23).
claims.span_hint: section: TPM for Measured Boot, first paragraph
claims.rationale: The authoritative source is the TCG platform profile PDF, which is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Linux TPM 2.0 driver support stabilized in kernel 4.9, `/dev/tpmrm0` arrived in kernel 4.12, and the surrounding package-version note includes unverified specifics.
claims.span_hint: section: Verifying TPM and Measured Boot on Kubernetes Nodes
claims.anchor_text: Linux TPM 2.0 driver support stabilized from kernel 4.9, with the `/dev/tpmrm0` resource-manager interface available from kernel 4.12. *(Note: The current version of the underlying `tpm2-tss` library cannot be confirmed via standard search results and must be checked directly on the GitHub releases page. Additionally, while secondary sources claim kernel 6.10 enabled `CONFIG_TCG_TPM2_HMAC` by default for TPM 2.0 session encryption, this remains unverified against primary kernel.org changelogs.)*
claims.suggested_rewrite: Modern Linux distributions commonly expose TPM device nodes such as `/dev/tpm0` and often `/dev/tpmrm0`; verify exact kernel and user-space package details against your distribution documentation and upstream release notes.
claims.rationale: The sentence mixes hard version claims with an explicit admission that adjacent version facts could not be verified.

claims.claim_text: Talos Linux has built-in TPM support for `systemDiskEncryption` with `tpm: {}` keys.
claims.span_hint: section: Enabling TPM in a Talos Linux Cluster
claims.rationale: Talos documentation directly supports this, but talos.dev is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: PKCS#11 v3.1 is the current OASIS Standard, and PKCS#11 v3.2 was published as Committee Specification 01 on November 14, 2025.
claims.span_hint: section: HashiCorp Vault with HSM Backend (PKCS#11), intro paragraph
claims.rationale: The section pool covers PKCS#11 v3.1, and the OASIS v3.2 page directly gives the Committee Specification 01 publication date.

claims.claim_text: Vault PKCS#11 HSM auto-unseal requires Vault Enterprise.
claims.span_hint: section: HashiCorp Vault with HSM Backend (PKCS#11), intro paragraph
claims.rationale: HashiCorp's PKCS#11 seal configuration page explicitly states that auto-unseal and seal wrapping for PKCS#11 require Vault Enterprise.

claims.claim_text: OpenBao has PKCS#11 seal support.
claims.span_hint: section: HashiCorp Vault with HSM Backend (PKCS#11), intro paragraph
claims.rationale: OpenBao's own documentation supports the claim, but openbao.org is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: YubiHSM 2 costs $650 and provides FIPS 140-2 Level 3 security in a USB form factor.
claims.span_hint: section: Using YubiHSM 2 for Smaller Deployments
claims.anchor_text: For clusters where a $50,000 network HSM is overkill, the YubiHSM 2 ($650) provides FIPS 140-2 Level 3 security in a USB form factor.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For smaller deployments, a USB HSM such as YubiHSM 2 can provide hardware-backed key storage at far lower cost than a network appliance, but you should verify current certifications and pricing directly with the vendor.
claims.rationale: This combines volatile pricing with vendor-specific certification detail that is not backed by an allowlisted primary source here.

claims.claim_text: A modified kernel changes PCR[8], prevents TPM release of the sealed LUKS key, keeps the disk encrypted, and automatically generates an alert.
claims.span_hint: section: What Happens on Tamper, paragraph after diagram
claims.anchor_text: *The modified kernel produces a different hash in PCR[8]. The LUKS key was sealed to the original PCR[8] value. The TPM will not release the key. The disk stays encrypted. The node fails to boot. An alert is generated.*
claims.suggested_rewrite: *A modified kernel produces a different PCR value than the one the key was sealed to, so the TPM does not release the key, the disk stays encrypted, and the node fails to boot until an operator intervenes.*
claims.rationale: The PCR/unseal behavior is the real lesson, but the claim that an alert is automatically generated is deployment-specific and unsupported.

claims.claim_text: Keylime is a CNCF project for remote boot attestation and runtime integrity measurement using TPM hardware, and it entered CNCF as a Sandbox project on September 22, 2020.
claims.span_hint: section: Remote Node Attestation, Keylime bullet
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page directly states Keylime's Sandbox acceptance date and describes its TPM-based attestation purpose.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE includes a built-in `tpm_devid` node attestor for TPMs provisioned with a DevID certificate.
claims.span_hint: section: Remote Node Attestation, SPIRE bullet
claims.rationale: SPIRE's official GitHub configuration reference lists `tpm_devid` as a built-in node attestor.

claims.claim_text: The community `bloomberg/spire-tpm-plugin` enables TPM 2.0 node attestation via TPM credential activation.
claims.span_hint: section: Remote Node Attestation, SPIRE bullet
claims.rationale: The repository README directly describes TPM credential activation as the attestation mechanism.

claims.claim_text: AKS Trusted Launch uses a TPM 2.0-compliant vTPM and measured boot / remote attestation for node VMs.
claims.span_hint: section: Remote Node Attestation, Cloud integration bullet
claims.rationale: Microsoft Learn directly states that Trusted Launch provides a TPM 2.0-compliant vTPM and measured-boot-based attestation.

claims.claim_text: FIPS 140-2 module validations remain on the CMVP active list through September 21, 2026, after which only FIPS 140-3 validations remain active.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, FIPS bullet
claims.rationale: The CMVP FAQ directly gives the active-list deadline and the switch to FIPS 140-3-only active validations.

claims.claim_text: After the historical-list cutoff, all new federal procurements require FIPS 140-3, and FIPS 140-3 adds physical security testing against fault injection attacks.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, FIPS bullet
claims.anchor_text: after that date all new federal procurements require FIPS 140-3. The new standard adds physical security testing against fault injection attacks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: FIPS 140-3 introduces updated non-invasive security requirements, and NIST CMVP transition and procurement guidance should be checked directly for the exact compliance implications in your environment.
claims.rationale: The sentence overstates procurement consequences and ties them to a specific testing characterization without a directly matching primary source.

claims.claim_text: cert-manager csi-driver v0.12.0 was released in February 2026 and native HSM/PKCS#11 key storage is still not implemented in the standard cert-manager CA issuer.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, cert-manager bullet
claims.anchor_text: - **cert-manager** csi-driver v0.12.0 was released in February 2026, but native HSM/PKCS#11 key storage is still not implemented in the standard cert-manager CA issuer. Backing CA keys with an HSM remains an open architectural challenge in standard Kubernetes deployments.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **cert-manager** standard issuer workflows do not provide first-class native HSM/PKCS#11-backed CA key storage, so HSM-backed CA designs still require additional components or custom integration choices.
claims.rationale: The exact release/date detail and the negative capability statement are not both cleanly backed here by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: The current TCG TPM 2.0 Library Specification is version 185 from March 2026, while the ISO standard remains anchored to ISO/IEC 11889:2015.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, TCG bullet
claims.rationale: The authoritative TCG source for the current revision is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Adi Shamir is the 'Shamir' in RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman).
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, Shamir bullet
claims.rationale: The IEEE Spectrum article explicitly identifies RSA as named for Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman.

claims.claim_text: Windows 11 requires TPM 2.0.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, Windows bullet
claims.rationale: Microsoft's Windows 11 requirements page directly lists TPM version 2.0 as a minimum hardware requirement.

claims.claim_text: Before 2021 many server vendors shipped TPM as a $50 add-on module, and now it is a non-negotiable standard on virtually all enterprise servers.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, Windows bullet
claims.anchor_text: Before 2021, many server vendors shipped TPM as a $50 add-on module. Now it is a non-negotiable standard on virtually all enterprise servers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Before Windows 11, TPM deployment on server and PC hardware was less uniform; today, TPM 2.0 support is far more common and is often treated as a baseline security feature.
claims.rationale: The sentence uses uncited pricing and sweeping market-share language that should be generalized.

further_reading.title: PKCS #11 Specification Version 3.1
further_reading.why_relevant: Normative reference for the PKCS#11 interface used by HSM-backed integrations.

further_reading.title: Encrypting Confidential Data at Rest
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative Kubernetes guidance for etcd encryption at rest and KMS provider integration points.

further_reading.title: Vault PKCS#11 Seal Configuration
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary vendor documentation for Vault's HSM-backed seal behavior and Enterprise requirement.

further_reading.title: Keylime CNCF Project Page
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful next read for remote attestation architecture after the TPM and measured-boot sections.

notes: Claims inside mermaid blocks, shell/code blocks, quiz prompts/answers, and the hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded per the task rules. Table rows were only surfaced where they carried hard vendor/pricing/throughput assertions.
